title: Philosophy
intro: Philosophy questions in quizbowl draw most heavily on the German tradition running from Immanuel Kant, the single most-asked answer in the category, through Georg Wilhelm Friedrich Hegel, Arthur Schopenhauer, and Friedrich Nietzsche, with Karl Marx and Soren Kierkegaard filling out the nineteenth century. Kant's categorical imperative, transcendental idealism, and Critique of Pure Reason recur constantly, both as direct answers and as background for questions on aesthetics, the sublime, and the analytic/synthetic distinction. Close behind are the British empiricists (David Hume, John Locke, George Berkeley, Thomas Hobbes) and the twentieth-century analytic tradition anchored by Ludwig Wittgenstein, Bertrand Russell, Gottlob Frege, and W. V. O. Quine, which together supply a steady stream of questions on logic, language, and epistemology.

Ancient Greek philosophy is the next-largest cluster, led by Aristotle, Plato, and Socrates, with the Hellenistic schools (Stoicism, Epicureanism, Skepticism, Cynicism) and the Pre-Socratics (Heraclitus, Parmenides, Democritus) supplying secondary answers. Medieval and Islamic philosophy appears mainly through Thomas Aquinas, William of Ockham, Avicenna, and Averroes, usually tied to scholastic method and the reconciliation of reason with faith. Continental philosophy after Kant splits into two well-represented lineages: phenomenology and existentialism (Heidegger, Husserl, Sartre, Merleau-Ponty, Beauvoir, Camus) and post-structuralism/critical theory (Foucault and Derrida lead, followed by Deleuze, Lacan, Adorno, Benjamin, Habermas, and the Frankfurt School).

Ethics and political philosophy form a distinct, frequently tested cluster spanning classical utilitarianism (Bentham, Mill), contemporary liberal and libertarian theory (Rawls, Nozick), and applied ethics (Singer, Nussbaum, the trolley problem), alongside recurring abstract-concept questions on justice, free will, causation, knowledge, truth, and consciousness that are not tied to a single philosopher. Non-Western traditions are a smaller but consistent presence, led by Confucius and Chinese thought (Mencius, Laozi, Zhuangzi, Mozi) with occasional questions on Indian (Nagarjuna, Buddhism) and African philosophy. Overall the canon skews heavily toward a handful of towering figures — Kant, Hume, Heidegger, Descartes, Wittgenstein, Aristotle, and Russell each appear dozens of times — with a very long tail of specific concepts, minor figures, and individual works appearing only a few times each.

sections.name: Ancient Greek Philosophy
sections.blurb: The Pre-Socratics, Socrates, Plato, Aristotle, and the Hellenistic schools that first framed metaphysics, ethics, and logic.

entries.note: Student of Plato who founded the Peripatetic school and formal logic; wrote the Nicomachean Ethics and Politics; theory of four causes.
Hellenistic school founded by Zeno of Citium teaching virtue and acceptance of fate; later exemplified by Seneca, Epictetus, and Marcus Aurelius.
Student of Socrates; theory of Forms, the philosopher-kings of the Republic, and the allegory of the cave.
Indivisible material particles posited by Democritus and Leucippus, later adopted in Epicurus's atomism.
Athenian philosopher who left no writings; the elenchus method; tried and executed on charges of impiety.
Examined as eros in Plato's Symposium, where Diotima's ladder ascends from bodies to the Form of Beauty.
Pre-Socratic of Elea; argued being is one and unchanging, and that change is mere illusion.
Founder of Epicureanism; pleasure as absence of pain, atomism, and dispelling fear of death and the gods.
Hellenistic school founded by Antisthenes, exemplified by Diogenes of Sinope's ascetic rejection of convention.
Aristotle's ethical treatise defining virtue as a mean between extremes, culminating in the contemplative life.
Itinerant Greek teachers of rhetoric such as Protagoras and Gorgias, criticized by Socrates for relativism over truth.
Pre-Socratic of flux ("no man steps in the same river twice") who identified the logos governing change.
Plato's dialogue on Socrates's last day, arguing for the soul's immortality before his execution.
Plato's dialogue on justice, the ideal city, and the philosopher-king, containing the allegory of the cave.
Pre-Socratic student of Parmenides; paradoxes of motion, including Achilles and the tortoise, defending monism.
Aristotle's classification of the basic kinds of being, such as substance and quality, later reworked by Kant.
Pre-Socratic who proposed the four classical elements, earth, air, fire, and water, governed by Love and Strife.
Pre-Socratic of Ephesus known for flux, the unity of opposites, and the governing logos.
Art of persuasion theorized in Aristotle's Rhetoric and contested by Socrates against the Sophists.
Subject of Plato's tripartite soul (reason, spirit, appetite) and Aristotle's soul as the form of a living body.
Character traits at the mean between excess and deficiency in Aristotle's Nicomachean Ethics.
Plato's dialogue asking whether the pious is loved by the gods because it is pious, or pious because loved.
Plato's dialogue on love (eros), featuring Aristophanes's myth of split humans and Diotima's ladder of love.
Roman statesman and Academic Skeptic who transmitted Greek philosophy to Latin readers in works like De Officiis.
Thales's proposed arche, or first principle, of all things, inaugurating Pre-Socratic natural philosophy.
Hellenistic school founded by Epicurus teaching pleasure and freedom from disturbance (ataraxia) as the good.
Greek biographer whose Lives of the Eminent Philosophers is a chief source on the ancient philosophers' lives.
Plato's dialogue asking whether virtue can be taught, featuring the slave-boy demonstration of recollection.
Greek term for intellect or reason; the highest human faculty in Aristotle, the second hypostasis after the One in Plotinus.
Ancient Pyrrhonist skeptic whose Outlines of Pyrrhonism systematized suspension of judgment (epoche).
Plato's dialogue examining and rejecting several definitions of knowledge, including true belief with an account.
Full epithet of the student of Plato who founded the Peripatetic school and formal logic.
Plato's dialogue where Socrates, awaiting execution, argues one must obey the state's laws despite an unjust verdict.
Aristotle's treatise classifying constitutions and arguing man is by nature a "political animal."
Plato's dialogue on cosmology, the demiurge, and the world's construction from geometric forms.
Central aesthetic concept; Plato's Form of Beauty in the Symposium and Kant's disinterested judgment of taste.
Plato's eternal, unchanging archetypes of which physical things are imperfect copies.
Heraclitus's term for the rational principle governing cosmic change, later central to Stoic physics.
Pre-Socratic who held numbers are the essence of reality; founded a religious-philosophical brotherhood.
Aristotle's deductive argument form, as in "all men are mortal," founding formal logic.
Hellenistic ideal of untroubled tranquility sought alike by Epicureans and Skeptics.
Greek term for flourishing or living well, the highest good in Aristotle's ethics.
Sophist rhetorician who argued nothing exists, or if it does, it cannot be known or communicated.
Greek medical theory, associated with Hippocrates and Galen, was closely intertwined with early natural philosophy.
Subject of Zeno's paradoxes questioning its coherence, and of Aristotle's physics of actuality and potentiality.
Plato's dialogue on rhetoric, madness, and love, containing the charioteer allegory of the soul.
Sophist known for "man is the measure of all things," a relativist epistemology.
Pre-Socratic who held numbers are the essence of reality; founded a religious-philosophical brotherhood.
Central concept of Aristotle's ethics, a disposition at the mean between excess and deficiency.
Aristotelian approach to ethics focused on character rather than rules or consequences, revived by Anscombe and MacIntyre.
Lucretius's Epicurean poem expounding atomism and denying divine intervention or an afterlife.
Cynic philosopher who reputedly lived in a barrel and mocked convention and Alexander the Great alike.
Pre-Socratic school of Parmenides and Zeno arguing reality is a single, unchanging being.
Roman Stoic ex-slave whose Enchiridion teaches focusing only on what is "up to us."
Argued for by Plato in the Phaedo through arguments from recollection and the soul's affinity with the Forms.
Full title of the Roman Stoic emperor-philosopher of the Meditations.
Marcus Aurelius's Stoic private reflections on virtue, mortality, and duty.
Concept of imitation central to Plato's critique of art and Aristotle's Poetics on tragedy.
Aristotle's school at the Lyceum, named for its walking style of teaching.
Aristotle's treatise on nature, motion, causation, and the unmoved mover.
Ancient skeptical school founded by Pyrrho advocating suspension of judgment (epoche) to achieve tranquility.
Subject of the "ship of Theseus" paradox questioning identity through the replacement of parts.
Aristotle's deductive argument form, as in "all men are mortal," founding formal logic.
Pre-Socratic who criticized anthropomorphic depictions of the gods and championed a single divine mind.
Athenian statesman and student of Socrates, featured in Plato's Symposium.
Plato's Republic image of prisoners mistaking shadows for reality, illustrating the ascent to knowledge of the Forms.
Anaximander's term for the "boundless" or indefinite substance underlying all things.
Metaphysical distinction traced to Parmenides and Plato, contrasting how things seem with how they truly are.
City home to the Cyrenaic school founded by Aristippus, which taught hedonism as the immediate pursuit of pleasure.
Pre-Socratic atomist who, with Leucippus, held that reality consists of indivisible atoms moving in the void.
Cynic philosopher of Sinope who reputedly lived in a barrel and mocked convention and Alexander the Great alike.
Greek term for common opinion or belief, contrasted by Plato with genuine knowledge, or episteme.
Southern Italian city home to the Eleatic school of Parmenides and Zeno.
Epictetus's Stoic manual on focusing only on what is "up to us."
Term for suspension of judgment used by ancient skeptics, later adapted by Husserl as phenomenological "bracketing."
View that pleasure is the sole intrinsic good, advocated by the Cyrenaics and, in refined form, the Epicureans.
View that mathematical objects exist as abstract, mind-independent entities, tracing to Plato's theory of Forms.
Figure from Greek tragedy sometimes invoked in philosophical discussions of fate, guilt, and self-knowledge.
Roman Stoic statesman and playwright whose letters and essays treat tranquility, mortality, and virtue.
First Pre-Socratic philosopher, who held water as the arche, or first principle, of all things.
Dramatic genre analyzed by Aristotle's Poetics as effecting catharsis through pity and fear.
Greek term for weakness of will, acting against one's own better judgment, discussed by Socrates and Aristotle.
Pre-Socratic who introduced nous, or mind, as the ordering principle of the cosmos.
Discussed in Seneca's On Anger as a passion to be mastered through Stoic reason.
Pre-Socratic doctrine of Democritus and Leucippus that reality consists of indivisible particles moving in the void.
Geometric figure invoked in Plato's theory of Forms as an example of a perfect, unattainable ideal.
Governed by Aristotle's law of non-contradiction, foundational to classical logic.
Aristotle's treatise on the soul as the form of a living body, distinguishing nutritive, sensitive, and rational functions.
Plato's brother and interlocutor in the Republic who poses the ring of Gyges challenge to justice.
Subject of the Euthyphro's dilemma questioning whether the pious is loved by the gods because pious, or pious because loved.
Aristotle's doctrine that substances are compounds of matter (hyle) and form (morphe).
Roman Epicurean poet whose De rerum natura expounds atomism and denies divine intervention.
Roman Stoic emperor whose private reflections form the Meditations.
Category including Zeno's paradoxes of motion and the liar paradox, testing the limits of logic and language.
Pre-Socratic of Elea who argued being is one and unchanging, and that change is mere illusion.
Central good in Epicurean and Cyrenaic ethics, analyzed critically by Aristotle as accompanying, not constituting, activity.
Famously banished from Plato's ideal city in the Republic for imitating rather than knowing truth.
Roman rhetorician whose Institutio Oratoria systematized classical rhetorical education.
Plato's theory that learning is remembering knowledge the soul possessed before birth, illustrated in the Meno.
View that truth or morality is relative to a perspective, associated with the Sophist Protagoras.
Plato's account of Socrates's trial defense against charges of impiety and corrupting the youth.
Plato's closing myth in the Republic depicting the afterlife's judgment and choice of reincarnated lives.
Aristotle's successor as head of the Lyceum, whose Characters catalogues moral types.
Aristotle's regress objection to Plato's theory of Forms, questioning what explains resemblance between Forms and particulars.
Sophist in Plato's Republic who argues justice is merely "the advantage of the stronger."
Name shared by Zeno of Elea, famed for paradoxes of motion, and Zeno of Citium, founder of Stoicism.
Likely references the "ship of Theseus" paradox questioning identity through the replacement of parts.
School founded by Plato in Athens, later home to the skeptical "Academic" tradition.
Plato's account of Socrates's trial defense against charges of impiety and corrupting the youth.
Aristotle's term for tragedy's purging of pity and fear in the audience.
Aristotle's four causes, material, formal, efficient, and final, explain why a thing exists or changes.
Earth, air, fire, and water, proposed by Empedocles as the fundamental constituents of matter.
Epicurean and Lucretian concept of an unpredictable swerve in falling atoms, introducing chance and free will into a deterministic universe.
Governed by Aristotle's law of non-contradiction, foundational to classical logic.
Literary form used by Plato to present philosophical arguments through conversational exchange.
Priestess who, in Plato's Symposium, teaches Socrates the ascending "ladder of love."
View that things have a fixed essence or nature, rooted in Aristotle's metaphysics.
Likely references Aristotle's four causes or the four classical elements.
Aristotle's material, formal, efficient, and final causes, explaining why a thing exists or changes.
Analyzed by Aristotle's Nicomachean Ethics as essential to the good life, distinguishing utility, pleasure, and virtue-based forms.
Dramatic genre analyzed by Aristotle's Poetics as effecting catharsis through pity and fear.
Likely references Plato's charioteer allegory of the soul in the Phaedrus, with its unruly and noble horses.
Concept traced to Anaximander's apeiron and central to Zeno's paradoxes of infinite divisibility.
Central to Plato's Republic, whose ideal city is ruled by philosopher-kings possessing knowledge of the Good.
Subject of Protagoras's dictum "man is the measure of all things."
Roman Stoic emperor's private reflections on virtue, mortality, and duty.
Pythagorean doctrine that celestial bodies produce a harmonious, mathematically ordered music through their motion.
Common title for Pre-Socratic treatises, including those of Parmenides and Heraclitus, on the fundamental nature of reality.
Aristotelian rhetorical appeal to emotion, alongside ethos and logos, in persuasive argument.
Greek term meaning "love of wisdom," coined in the tradition of Pythagoras and Socrates.
Plato's dialogue on justice, the ideal city, and the philosopher-king, containing the allegory of the cave.
Aristotle's treatise on tragedy, catharsis, and the elements of dramatic narrative.
Subject of Plato's critique in the Republic as a corrupting imitation, and Aristotle's Poetics as a legitimate art.
Stoic term, from Epictetus, for the faculty of rational choice, the only thing truly "up to us."
Founder of Pyrrhonism, the ancient skeptical school advocating suspension of judgment for tranquility.
Ancient skeptical tradition, systematized by Sextus Empiricus, advocating suspension of judgment (epoche).
Aristotle's example in De Interpretatione questioning whether future contingent statements are already true or false.
Paradox questioning identity through the replacement of parts of a vessel over time.
Aristotelian category, with genus, for classifying kinds of things by their essential differences.
First Pre-Socratic philosopher, who held water as the arche, or first principle, of all things.
Plato's Republic image of prisoners mistaking shadows for reality, illustrating the ascent to knowledge of the Forms.
Euclid's foundational geometric text, whose axiomatic method inspired Spinoza's Ethics.
Personified in Plato's Crito, arguing Socrates must obey the state's verdict despite its injustice.
Aristophanes's satirical name for Socrates's school in the comedy The Clouds.
Athenian proceeding in which Socrates was condemned to death on charges of impiety and corrupting the youth.
Aristotle's example of matter, or hyle, that takes on form in his hylomorphic theory of substance.
Founder of Stoicism, teaching virtue, reason, and acceptance of fate.
Arguments, such as Achilles and the tortoise, against the coherence of motion and plurality.

sections.name: Medieval & Islamic-Jewish Philosophy
sections.blurb: Patristic and Scholastic thought alongside the Islamic and Jewish philosophers who preserved and extended the classical inheritance.

entries.note: English scholastic nominalist; Ockham's razor favors the simplest sufficient explanation.
Late-antique school founded by Plotinus positing emanation from the One; influenced Christian and Islamic thought.
Persian polymath (Ibn Sina); flying man argument and the essence-existence distinction; influenced scholasticism.
Scottish scholastic "Subtle Doctor"; haecceity (individuating "thisness") and the univocity of being.
Dominican scholastic; synthesized Aristotle with Christian theology in the Summa Theologica; the Five Ways and natural law.
Andalusian Islamic philosopher (Ibn Rushd); commentaries on Aristotle defending philosophy's compatibility with faith.
Subject of ontological, cosmological, and teleological arguments for existence central to medieval scholastic philosophy.
Anselm's a priori proof that God, as the greatest conceivable being, must exist.
Dominican scholastic who synthesized Aristotle with Christian theology in the Summa Theologica.
French scholastic logician known for Sic et Non and his tragic love affair with Heloise.
Dominican scholastic who synthesized Aristotle with Christian theology in the Summa Theologica.
Aquinas's synthesis of Aristotelian philosophy and Christian doctrine, including the Five Ways proving God's existence.
French scholastic logician whose "Buridan's ass" illustrates a paradox of rational choice under perfect indifference.
Medieval Jewish philosopher whose Guide for the Perplexed reconciles Aristotelian reason with Torah.
Home to Roman Stoicism, medieval scholastics like Aquinas and Boethius, and Renaissance humanists like Pico della Mirandola.
Patristic philosopher-theologian of the Confessions and City of God, on grace, time, and the two cities.
Persian Islamic theologian whose Incoherence of the Philosophers critiqued Avicennan rationalism with occasionalist skepticism.
Late Roman philosopher whose Consolation of Philosophy, written awaiting execution, bridges antiquity and scholasticism.
Subject of theodicy, reconciling an omnipotent, benevolent God with the existence of suffering.
Scholastic position, associated with Ockham, denying universals exist apart from particular things or names.
Founder of Neoplatonism; emanation of Nous and Soul from the ineffable One.
Neoplatonist student of Plotinus whose Isagoge introduced Aristotle's categories to medieval logic.
Theodicy question of reconciling an omnipotent, benevolent God with the existence of suffering.
Patristic philosopher-theologian of the Confessions and City of God, on grace, time, and the two cities.
Language of the medieval Islamic philosophical tradition (falsafa) that transmitted and expanded Greek philosophy.
Islamic "Second Teacher" after Aristotle, who synthesized Platonic political philosophy with Islamic thought.
Full name of the late Roman philosopher of The Consolation of Philosophy, written awaiting execution.
Framework for much medieval philosophy reconciling faith and reason, from Augustine to Aquinas.
Duns Scotus's term for the individuating "thisness" that makes a particular unique.
Tradition whose medieval philosophers, including Maimonides, reconciled Aristotelian reason with scripture.
Site of Al-Andalus's flourishing of Islamic and Jewish philosophy, home to Averroes and Maimonides.
Theological-philosophical concept of the afterlife discussed within medieval Christian, Islamic, and Jewish thought.
Principle of parsimony, favoring the simplest sufficient explanation, attributed to William of Ockham.
Medieval method combining logical analysis with Christian theology, exemplified by Aquinas and Duns Scotus.
Scholastic theologian who formulated the ontological argument for God's existence.
"The Philosopher of the Arabs," the first major Islamic Peripatetic philosopher to synthesize Greek philosophy with Islamic theology.
Argument, developed in Aquinas's Five Ways, inferring a first cause or unmoved mover for the universe's existence.
Irish Neoplatonist scholastic whose Periphyseon proposed a fourfold division of nature.
Islamic rational theology whose cosmological argument holds that whatever begins to exist has a cause.
Medieval scholastic whose Sentences became the standard theology textbook commented on by generations of scholastics.
Patristic philosopher-theologian of the Confessions and City of God, on grace, time, and the two cities.
Boethius's work, written awaiting execution, personifying Philosophy's consolation on fortune and providence.
Maimonides's work reconciling Aristotelian philosophy with Jewish scripture and law.
Attempt to justify God's goodness given the world's suffering, notably Leibniz's "best of all possible worlds."

entries.topic: Abu Hamid Muhammad ibn Muhammad al-Ghazali
entries.answerline: Abu Hamid Muhammad ibn Muhammad al-Ghazali
entries.note: Full name of the Persian Islamic theologian whose Incoherence of the Philosophers critiqued Avicennan rationalism.
Subject of scholastic debate over immaterial intelligences, notably Aquinas's discussion of angelic individuation.
Aquinas's principle distinguishing intended from merely foreseen harmful effects of an action.
Subject of ontological, cosmological, and teleological proofs debated from Anselm and Aquinas onward.
Likely references Aquinas's "Five Ways," the five arguments for God's existence in the Summa Theologica.
Islamic historian-philosopher whose Muqaddimah theorizes the cyclical rise and fall of dynasties through asabiyyah, or group solidarity.
Arabic name of Avicenna, the Persian polymath known for the flying man argument.
Medieval theory of motion, associated with Buridan, positing an internal force imparted to a moving body, anticipating inertia.
Scholastic and Islamic philosophical concept of the rational faculty, notably Averroes's controversial unity-of-intellect thesis.
Site of Al-Andalus's flourishing of Islamic and Jewish philosophy, home to Averroes and Maimonides.
Language of medieval Western scholastic philosophy and its translations of Aristotle and Arabic commentators.
German cardinal-philosopher known for "learned ignorance" and the coincidence of opposites in the infinite God.
Neoplatonist student of Plotinus whose Isagoge introduced Aristotle's categories to medieval logic.
Central subject of philosophy of religion, examining arguments for God's existence and the problem of evil.
Spanish scholastic scholars who developed early natural law and economic theory extending Aquinas's thought.
Mystical tradition within Islam seeking direct experiential knowledge of God, associated with Al-Ghazali's later thought.
Plotinus's ineffable source of all being in Neoplatonic emanation.
Possibly referencing the Christian doctrine of the Trinity debated by medieval theologians.
Duns Scotus's concept that "being" applies in the same sense to God and to creatures.
Contemporary Christian philosopher who revived and popularized the kalam cosmological argument.
German scholastic and teacher of Aquinas who championed Aristotelian natural philosophy within Christian theology.
Method of applying general moral principles to particular cases, prominent in scholastic and Jesuit ethics.
Tradition of rational defense of Christian doctrine, from Augustine and Aquinas to modern figures like C. S. Lewis.
Late Neoplatonist who emphasized theurgy, ritual practice aimed at unifying the soul with the divine.
Arabic name of Averroes, whose commentaries on Aristotle defended philosophy's compatibility with faith.
Porphyry's introduction to Aristotle's Categories, foundational to medieval logic's "tree of Porphyry."
Tradition whose medieval philosophers, including Maimonides, reconciled Aristotelian reason with scripture.
Principle of parsimony, favoring the simplest sufficient explanation, attributed to William of Ockham.
Divine attribute debated in medieval theology, raising paradoxes like whether God can create a stone too heavy to lift.
Group of 14th-century Merton College scholastics who applied mathematical reasoning to physical problems of motion.
Hellenistic Jewish philosopher who synthesized Platonic philosophy with Jewish scripture through allegorical interpretation.
English Franciscan scholastic and early advocate of the experimental method and optics within scholastic natural philosophy.
Scholastic theologian who formulated the ontological argument for God's existence.
Franciscan scholastic theologian whose The Journey of the Mind to God traces an Augustinian ascent to divine illumination.
Abelard's work juxtaposing contradictory authoritative texts to train dialectical reasoning.
Medieval scholastic center where figures like Aquinas and Buridan taught and studied.

sections.name: Renaissance & Early Modern Rationalism
sections.blurb: Continental rationalists and Renaissance political thinkers who grounded knowledge in reason and mathematical method.

entries.note: French rationalist; "cogito ergo sum," mind-body dualism, and methodical doubt in the Meditations; father of modern philosophy.
Dutch rationalist; pantheist monism argued geometrically in the Ethics; conatus and "deus sive natura."
German rationalist; monads, pre-established harmony, and the principle of sufficient reason; co-invented calculus.
French mathematician-philosopher; Pascal's wager and the Pensees; "the heart has its reasons."
Descartes's 1641 work using methodical doubt to establish the cogito and prove God's existence.
German rationalist; monads and the "best of all possible worlds," a theodicy against the problem of evil.
Florentine political theorist; The Prince argues rulers may need to act immorally to preserve the state.
French rationalist occasionalist who held God is the only true cause, with all things seen "in God."
Doctrine, especially Malebranche's, that God is the sole causal agent and creatures are mere occasions for divine action.
Renaissance movement emphasizing human dignity and classical learning, exemplified by Pico della Mirandola.
Leibniz's principle that everything must have a reason or cause explaining why it is so and not otherwise.
Italian philosopher whose The New Science proposed a cyclical philosophy of history opposing Cartesian rationalism.
German rationalist known for monads and the principle of sufficient reason.
Thomas More's work depicting an ideal communal society, coining the term "utopia."
French Renaissance essayist known for the skeptical motto "Que sais-je?", or "What do I know?"
Leibniz's simple, indivisible substances that make up reality, each reflecting the universe from its own perspective.
French skeptic whose Historical and Critical Dictionary influenced Enlightenment religious skepticism.
Spinoza's work arguing for freedom of thought and a naturalistic, historical-critical reading of scripture.
Full title of Spinoza's Ethics, structured with axioms and propositions in the manner of Euclid.
Renaissance humanist whose Oration on the Dignity of Man celebrates human free will and unbounded potential.
Renaissance Platonist who translated Plato into Latin and led the Florentine Academy.

entries.topic: Oration on the Dignity of Man
entries.answerline: Oration on the Dignity of Man
entries.note: Pico della Mirandola's Renaissance manifesto on humanity's freedom to shape its own nature.
Machiavelli's treatise advising rulers that political necessity may override conventional morality.
English rationalist whose monistic vitalist metaphysics in Principles of the Most Ancient and Modern Philosophy influenced Leibniz.
Pascal's decision-theoretic argument that believing in God is the rational bet given infinite potential reward.
French Jansenist philosopher-logician who co-authored the Port-Royal Logic and corresponded critically with Descartes and Leibniz.
Model of certain knowledge for Descartes and Spinoza, who structured the Ethics in geometric, axiomatic order.
View identifying God with the whole of nature, most famously Spinoza's "deus sive natura."
French priest-philosopher who revived Epicurean atomism and objected to Descartes's Meditations.
Metaphysical category of independently existing things, central to Descartes's dualism and Spinoza's single substance.
Vico's work proposing a cyclical philosophy of history opposing Cartesian rationalism.
Descartes's cogito, the indubitable foundation of knowledge established through methodical doubt.
Machiavelli's republican work analyzing Roman history to draw lessons on liberty and civic virtue.
Machiavelli's concept of unpredictable circumstance, or fortuna, that a ruler must master through virtu.
Renaissance philosopher who advocated an infinite universe with countless worlds; burned at the stake for heresy.
Home to Spinoza's rationalist philosophy and a haven for Descartes's exile writings.
Italian Renaissance friar-philosopher whose The City of the Sun depicts a utopian communal society.
Machiavelli's term for a ruler's skill and boldness in mastering fortune.
Descartes's criterion for certain knowledge: ideas perceived by the mind with clarity and distinctness.
Pascal's remark that history would have changed had Cleopatra's nose been shorter, illustrating contingency's role in events.
Spinoza's term for each thing's innate striving to persevere in its own being.
Early modern mechanistic theory, associated with Boyle and Locke, explaining matter as composed of tiny particles.
Descartes's work introducing methodical doubt and the cogito in accessible, autobiographical form.
Central to Descartes's Meditations, where the dream argument questions whether waking experience can be distinguished from dreaming.
French Enlightenment natural philosopher who translated and commented on Newton's Principia.
Descartes's hypothetical deceiver used in the Meditations to motivate radical, methodical doubt.
French Catholic theological movement, associated with Port-Royal and Pascal, emphasizing predestination and grace.
English natural philosopher who proposed a materialist, vitalist metaphysics challenging Cartesian dualism.
Full name of the Florentine political theorist of The Prince.
Pascal's posthumously assembled fragments including the wager and "the heart has its reasons."
Leibniz's principle that God, being perfect, created the best of all possible worlds.
Descartes's proposed site of mind-body interaction in his dualist theory.
French Jansenist convent associated with Pascal and Arnauld's Port-Royal Logic.
Subject of Machiavelli's The Prince, advising rulers on acquiring and maintaining power.
English statesman whose Utopia depicts an ideal communal society, coining the term "utopia."
Metaphysical category of independently existing things, central to Descartes's dualism and Spinoza's single substance.
Descartes's "I think, therefore I am," the indubitable foundation of knowledge.
Leibniz's principle that no two distinct things can share all the same properties.

sections.name: British Empiricism & Enlightenment
sections.blurb: Empiricist epistemology and Enlightenment social-contract theory from Britain and the continent.

entries.note: Scottish empiricist skeptical of induction and necessary causation; reason is "slave of the passions"; formulated the is-ought problem.
English empiricist; tabula rasa in An Essay Concerning Human Understanding; natural rights and social contract theory.
Irish empiricist and idealist bishop; "esse est percipi" denies matter exists apart from perception.
English philosopher; Leviathan describes escaping the state of nature's "war of all against all" via social contract.
Genevan Enlightenment thinker; The Social Contract's general will and the natural goodness corrupted by society.
English Enlightenment feminist; A Vindication of the Rights of Woman argues for women's education and rationality.
Epistemological view that knowledge derives from sense experience, developed by Locke, Berkeley, and Hume.
Locke's 1689 work arguing the mind is a tabula rasa filled in by experience.
English philosopher; founder of the inductive scientific method, warned against the "idols" that mislead the mind.
18th-century movement championing reason, science, and individual rights against tradition and religious authority.
Hobbes's 1651 work justifying absolute sovereignty as the remedy to the state of nature's war of all against all.
French Enlightenment writer; Candide satirizes philosophical optimism; advocate of deism and religious tolerance.
Hume's restatement of his empiricist epistemology, skeptical of induction and of testimony for miracles.
Subject of Hume's argument that testimony for a miracle is always outweighed by uniform experience of natural law.
Scottish Enlightenment philosopher-economist; The Wealth of Nations and The Theory of Moral Sentiments's impartial spectator.
Home to the Scottish Enlightenment figures Hume, Smith, Reid, and Hutcheson.
Adam Smith's work grounding morality in sympathy and the judgment of an "impartial spectator."
17th-century English group, including Cudworth and More, blending Platonism with rationalist theology against Hobbesian materialism.
Hobbes's earlier political work outlining the social contract that preceded Leviathan.
French Enlightenment philosophe; chief editor of the Encyclopedie.
Political upheaval that Burke criticized in his Reflections and that tested Enlightenment ideals of liberty.
Hume's posthumous work critiquing the design argument for God's existence through dialogue.
English philosopher who coined "survival of the fittest" and applied evolutionary theory to society as Social Darwinism.
Full epithet of the English philosopher of Leviathan and the social contract.
Scottish "common sense" philosopher who opposed Hume's skepticism with a direct realist epistemology.

entries.topic: Charles-Louis de Secondat, Baron de Montesquieu
entries.answerline: Charles-Louis de Secondat, Baron de Montesquieu
entries.note: Full name of the French Enlightenment theorist of the separation of powers.
Early English feminist philosopher whose "A Serious Proposal to the Ladies" argued for women's education and rationality.
French Enlightenment political theorist of the separation of powers in The Spirit of the Laws.
Locke's work arguing for religious tolerance and the separation of church and state.

entries.topic: A Vindication of the Rights of Woman
entries.answerline: A Vindication of the Rights of Woman
entries.note: Wollstonecraft's work arguing for women's education and rationality.
French Enlightenment political theorist of the separation of powers in The Spirit of the Laws.
Enlightenment religious view holding a rational God created the universe but does not intervene in it.
Subject of Locke's and Hume's major epistemological works examining the scope and limits of the mind.
Hume's term for vivid, immediate sense perceptions, as opposed to fainter derivative "ideas."
Hume's observation that descriptive premises cannot alone entail a prescriptive conclusion.
Locke's category of properties, like color and taste, that exist only as effects on a perceiver, unlike primary qualities.
Mandeville's satirical work arguing private vices produce public benefits.
Enlightenment value defended by Locke's Letter Concerning Toleration and Voltaire's advocacy for religious tolerance.
English theologian whose watchmaker analogy argues design in nature implies a divine designer.
Hume's early masterwork on understanding, the passions, and morals, grounding all knowledge in experience.

entries.topic: Anthony Ashley Cooper, 3rd Earl of Shaftesbury
entries.answerline: Anthony Ashley Cooper, 3rd Earl of Shaftesbury
entries.note: English philosopher who argued for an innate moral sense and aesthetic harmony, influencing Hutcheson.
Rousseau's treatise on natural child development and education free from corrupting social convention.
Home to the empiricist tradition of Locke, Berkeley, and Hume's predecessors, and to Hobbes and Bentham.
Rousseau's concept of the collective will aimed at the common good, underlying legitimate law in The Social Contract.
Full title of the Irish empiricist and idealist who denied matter exists apart from perception.

entries.topic: Leviathan: Or, the Matter, Forme, and Power of a Commonwealth Ecclesiasticall and Civil
entries.answerline: Leviathan: Or, the Matter, Forme, and Power of a Commonwealth Ecclesiasticall and Civil
entries.note: Full title of Hobbes's 1651 work justifying absolute sovereignty via the social contract.
Rousseauian concept of humans as naturally good before corruption by civil society.
Hume's term for emotions and desires, which he held govern reason rather than the reverse.
Subject of Locke's Letter Concerning Toleration, arguing for religious tolerance and separation of church and state.
English rationalist theologian who debated Leibniz over absolute space and time in the Leibniz-Clarke correspondence.

sections.name: German Idealism & 19th-Century Philosophy
sections.blurb: Kant through Hegel, Schopenhauer, Kierkegaard, Marx, and Nietzsche, and the systems and reactions that followed the critical turn.

entries.note: Prussian Enlightenment philosopher; categorical imperative and transcendental idealism in the Critique of Pure Reason; founder of German Idealism.
German idealist of dialectical logic (thesis-antithesis-synthesis) and the master-slave dialectic; wrote The Phenomenology of Spirit.
Pessimist German philosopher of The World as Will and Representation; drew on Kant and Buddhism; influenced Nietzsche.
German philosopher of will to power and eternal recurrence; declared "God is dead"; the Ubermensch in Thus Spoke Zarathustra.
German philosopher and economist; historical materialism and class struggle in The Communist Manifesto and Capital.
Study of beauty and art systematized by Kant's Critique of Judgment, which distinguishes the beautiful from the sublime.
Philosophy of history examines historical meaning and progress, notably Hegel's dialectical unfolding of Spirit through time.
View that reality is fundamentally mind-dependent; Berkeley's subjective form, Kant's transcendental form, Hegel's absolute form.
Kant's 1781 work reconciling rationalism and empiricism through transcendental idealism and synthetic a priori knowledge.
Kant's supreme moral principle: act only on maxims one could will as universal law, treating persons as ends.
German idealist between Kant and Hegel; the Wissenschaftslehre grounds reality in the self-positing "I."
Danish "father of existentialism"; the leap of faith and life-stages in Fear and Trembling and Either/Or.
Hegel's 1807 work tracing consciousness's dialectical development toward Absolute Knowing, including the master-slave dialectic.
Kierkegaard's work on Abraham's binding of Isaac and the "teleological suspension of the ethical."
Kierkegaard's work contrasting the aesthetic and ethical stages of life through pseudonymous authors.
Full name of the German philosopher of will to power, eternal recurrence, and the death of God.
Aesthetic category of overwhelming greatness distinguished from the beautiful by Burke and Kant.
Nietzsche's work tracing "good and evil" and "good and bad" through master and slave morality.
Full name of the Danish "father of existentialism," known for the leap of faith and Fear and Trembling.
Neo-Kantian German philosopher; The Philosophy of Symbolic Forms treats myth, language, and science as symbolic systems.
German philosopher whose The Essence of Christianity argued God is a projection of human nature, influencing Marx.
German-Jewish Enlightenment philosopher who defended rational religion and Jewish emancipation; friend of Lessing.
Kant's third critique on aesthetic and teleological judgment, distinguishing the beautiful and the sublime.

entries.topic: Elements of the Philosophy of Right
entries.answerline: Elements of the Philosophy of Right
entries.note: Hegel's work on ethical life (Sittlichkeit), civil society, and the rational state.
Home to Kant, Hegel, Schopenhauer, and Nietzsche, and to the phenomenological and Frankfurt School traditions.
Subject of Kant's controversial claim that lying is wrong even to a murderer seeking a victim.
Regarded by Schopenhauer as the most direct expression of the metaphysical Will, and prized in Nietzsche's Birth of Tragedy.
Central concept of Kierkegaard's Repetition, contrasted with Platonic recollection.

entries.topic: The World as Will and Representation
entries.answerline: The World as Will and Representation
entries.note: Schopenhauer's magnum opus positing an irrational, striving Will underlying all appearance.
Method of reasoning through contradiction toward higher truth, central to Hegel's philosophy and Marx's materialist inversion of it.

entries.topic: Groundwork of the Metaphysics of Morals
entries.answerline: Groundwork of the Metaphysics of Morals
entries.note: Kant's foundational ethical work introducing the categorical imperative.
Subject of Kierkegaard's dissertation The Concept of Irony, examining Socratic irony as indirect communication.
Schelling's Naturphilosophie treated nature as a developing, quasi-spiritual organism.
View that life lacks inherent meaning or value, diagnosed by Nietzsche as a consequence of "the death of God."
Marx's term for the industrial working class destined, in his theory, to overthrow capitalism.
Site of a distinct 19th-century philosophical tradition split between Slavophiles and Westernizers, influenced by German idealism.
Kierkegaard's work analyzing despair as a misrelation of the self, "the sickness unto death."
Subject of aesthetics; Hegel treats art as a stage of Absolute Spirit, while Benjamin analyzes its mechanical reproduction.
Nietzsche's work critiquing traditional morality "beyond" conventional categories of good and evil.
Economic system analyzed by Marx as generating alienation and exploitation of the proletariat's labor.
Kierkegaard's "leap of faith" beyond rational justification, exemplified by Abraham in Fear and Trembling.
German philosopher and Marx's collaborator; co-wrote The Communist Manifesto and funded Marx's work.
Marxist concept of theory realized through practical, transformative action.
Central faculty debated across rationalism, empiricism, and Kant's critique of its proper scope and limits.
Marx and Engels's 1848 pamphlet declaring "the history of all hitherto existing society is the history of class struggles."
Schopenhauer's metaphysical Will, an irrational thing-in-itself underlying all phenomena.
Subject of aesthetics debated by Hegel, Heidegger in "The Origin of the Work of Art," and Benjamin.
Biblical patriarch whose binding of Isaac Kierkegaard analyzes as a "teleological suspension of the ethical."
Italian idealist philosopher, influenced by Hegel, who defined art as intuition and expression.

entries.topic: Beyond Good and Evil: Prelude to a Philosophy of the Future
entries.answerline: Beyond Good and Evil: Prelude to a Philosophy of the Future
entries.note: Full title of Nietzsche's work critiquing traditional morality beyond conventional good and evil.
British absolute idealist whose Appearance and Reality argued ordinary experience is self-contradictory and only the Absolute is fully real.
Variant of the name of the German idealist of dialectical logic and the master-slave dialectic.
Nationality shared by Kant, Hegel, Schopenhauer, and Nietzsche, whose idealist and post-idealist systems dominate the 19th century.
German philosopher of language and history, a precursor to Romanticism who emphasized cultural particularity, or Volksgeist.
German philosopher of language and history, a precursor to Romanticism who emphasized cultural particularity, or Volksgeist.
Political-philosophical tradition founded by Marx and Engels analyzing history through class struggle and historical materialism.
Nietzsche's work containing the parable of the madman proclaiming "God is dead."
Nietzsche's prophetic mouthpiece in Thus Spoke Zarathustra, proclaiming the Ubermensch and eternal recurrence.
Marx's concept of workers' estrangement from their labor, its products, and their own human nature under capitalism.
Marx's economic critique of capitalism analyzing surplus value and commodity fetishism.
German philosopher who accused Spinoza's rationalism of leading to atheism, sparking the Pantheism Controversy.
Kant's ideal moral community where all persons are treated as ends, never merely as means.
Hegel's account in the Phenomenology of Spirit of self-consciousness emerging through a struggle for recognition.
German philosopher whose The Ego and Its Own advocates radical individualist egoism against all abstractions, including the state and morality.
Hegelian concept of mutual acknowledgment between self-consciousnesses, central to the master-slave dialectic.
German composer whose Gesamtkunstwerk ideal was championed, then rejected, by Nietzsche.
Hegel's Geist, the collective, self-developing consciousness that unfolds through history toward self-knowledge.
Feuerbach's work arguing God is a projection of human nature, influencing Marx.
Marx and Engels's work articulating historical materialism and the idea that ruling ideas reflect the ruling class.
Nietzsche's proclamation, in The Gay Science, of the decline of religious belief's cultural authority.
Kant's essay defining Enlightenment as humanity's emergence from self-imposed immaturity.
Nietzsche's ideal of "love of fate," affirming and embracing one's entire life, including its suffering.
Discussed in Hegel's aesthetics as the earliest and most "symbolic" of the fine arts.
Subject of aesthetics debated by Hegel, Heidegger, and Benjamin over art's truth-content and reproducibility.
German rationalist systematizer of Leibniz's philosophy who shaped 18th-century academic terminology.
Marx's economic critique of capitalism analyzing surplus value and commodity fetishism.
German poet-philosopher whose On the Aesthetic Education of Man argues art can reconcile reason and sensuous nature.
German idealist between Fichte and Hegel whose Naturphilosophie treats nature as developing spirit.
Central category of Ernst Bloch's The Principle of Hope, theorizing utopian longing as a driving historical force.
German counter-Enlightenment philosopher, "the Magus of the North," who critiqued Kant's rationalism from a faith-based standpoint.
Central topic of Kant's third Critique, distinguishing determinative from reflective judgment in aesthetics and teleology.
Russian Marxist revolutionary and theorist of "permanent revolution," rival of Stalin.
Subject of Kant's controversial claim that lying is wrong even to a murderer seeking a victim.
Schopenhauer's view that suffering outweighs happiness in existence, given the irrational, striving Will.
Kant's concept of humanity's innate propensity to prioritize self-interest over the moral law.
Nietzsche's concept of a repressed resentment that, in slave morality, inverts the values of the powerful.
British idealist philosopher and historian whose The Idea of History argues understanding the past requires re-enacting past thought.
Kierkegaard's work elaborating the aesthetic, ethical, and religious stages of existence.
Marxist term for a society's political, legal, and cultural institutions, held to be shaped by its economic base.

entries.topic: The Birth of Tragedy From the Spirit of Music
entries.answerline: The Birth of Tragedy From the Spirit of Music
entries.note: Nietzsche's early work contrasting Apollonian order with Dionysian excess in Greek tragedy.
Nietzsche's proclamation, in The Gay Science, of the decline of religious belief's cultural authority.
Kant's noumenal reality as it exists independent of human perception, unknowable in itself.
Kant's argument in the Critique of Pure Reason justifying the objective validity of the categories of understanding.
Nietzsche's ideal of a self-overcoming figure who creates new values beyond conventional morality.

sections.name: Ethics & Political Philosophy
sections.blurb: Utilitarian, deontological, and contractarian theories of right action, justice, and the state, ancient to contemporary.

entries.note: Consequentialist theory holding the right act maximizes aggregate happiness; developed by Jeremy Bentham and John Stuart Mill.
English utilitarian; On Liberty defends individual freedom via the harm principle; refined Bentham's hedonic calculus.
German-American political theorist; The Origins of Totalitarianism and the "banality of evil" in Eichmann in Jerusalem.
American political philosopher; A Theory of Justice's veil of ignorance and original position define justice as fairness.
Australian utilitarian ethicist; Animal Liberation and effective altruism; arguments against speciesism.
Branch of philosophy studying right action; deontological, consequentialist, and virtue-based approaches.
American libertarian political philosopher; Anarchy, State, and Utopia defends the minimal state against Rawls.
Central political concept concerning fair distribution and desert; from Plato's Republic to Rawls's justice as fairness.
English founder of modern utilitarianism; felicific calculus and the "greatest happiness principle"; designed the panopticon.
American philosopher; capabilities approach to justice developed with Amartya Sen; work on emotion and virtue ethics.
British ethicist; Reasons and Persons on personal identity, rationality, and population ethics.
German-American political philosopher; esoteric writing and critique of modern relativism revived classical political thought.
Thought experiment by Foot and Thomson contrasting killing and letting die to test consequentialist versus deontological intuitions.
Irish-British statesman; founder of modern conservatism who critiqued the French Revolution in his Reflections.
Applied-ethics debate over fetal personhood and bodily autonomy; Judith Jarvis Thomson's violinist thought experiment.
British philosopher whose "Modern Moral Philosophy" spurred the revival of virtue ethics; student of Wittgenstein.
Animal ethics debates moral status and rights, notably through Peter Singer's argument against speciesism.
British political theorist whose "Two Concepts of Liberty" distinguishes negative from positive freedom.
Concept from Bernard Williams and Nagel that moral judgment depends partly on factors outside an agent's control.
Mill's 1859 defense of individual freedom, free speech, and the harm principle against social tyranny.
Political philosophy rejecting the state as illegitimate, from Proudhon and Bakunin to Kropotkin.
Irish "father of the Scottish Enlightenment"; moral sense theory and an early phrasing of the "greatest happiness" principle.
Subject of just war theory, tracing the conditions for justly entering and conducting armed conflict.
British ethicist who critiqued utilitarianism's demand for impartiality via "moral luck" and personal integrity.
Philosophical topic on cultivating virtue and knowledge in the young, central to Plato's Republic and Rousseau's Emile.
American ethicist whose violinist analogy defends abortion rights; key voice in trolley-problem literature.
British ethicist who originated the trolley problem and helped revive virtue ethics.
Jurisprudential view, associated with Austin and Hart, that law's validity depends on social facts rather than moral merit.
Rawls's 1971 work proposing justice as fairness via the original position and veil of ignorance.
Dutch jurist, "father of international law," who grounded natural law theory in On the Law of War and Peace.
Political concept split by Isaiah Berlin into negative and positive senses; central to Mill's On Liberty.
Political theory that legitimate authority derives from an agreement among individuals, developed by Hobbes, Locke, and Rousseau.
Nozick's libertarian response to Rawls, defending a minimal "night-watchman" state.
Ethical theory judging actions solely by their outcomes; utilitarianism is its most prominent form.
Political philosophy favoring tradition and gradual change, articulated by Edmund Burke against revolutionary upheaval.
Authoritarian ultranationalist ideology analyzed critically by theorists including Umberto Eco and the Frankfurt School.
British utilitarian whose Methods of Ethics rigorously compared egoism, intuitionism, and utilitarianism.
Political-philosophical topic on ownership rights, from Locke's labor theory to Proudhon's "property is theft."
Anscombe's 1958 paper arguing modern ethics should abandon "moral obligation" for Aristotelian virtue.
German political theorist who defined the political by the friend-enemy distinction; theorist of sovereignty and the "state of exception."
Full name of the British philosopher whose "Modern Moral Philosophy" spurred the revival of virtue ethics.
Doctrine, rooted in Aquinas and Grotius, setting conditions for justly entering and conducting war.
Political-philosophical concept of entitlements, developed through natural-rights and human-rights traditions.
American legal philosopher who framed law as "integrity," opposing legal positivism.
Political concept analyzed by Arendt as a modern form of rule effacing the distinction between public and private.
Political system examined from Plato's critique of mob rule to Dewey's participatory ideal.
Central ethical concept debated across metaethics, from Moore's "naturalistic fallacy" to Aristotelian eudaimonia.
Full name of the American political philosopher of A Theory of Justice.
Moore's charge that defining "good" in terms of natural properties commits a logical fallacy.
Rawls's hypothetical choice situation behind the "veil of ignorance," used to derive principles of justice.
Applied-ethics topic debated in feminist philosophy over speech, harm, and objectification.
Parfit's work on personal identity, rationality, and obligations to future generations.
Enlightenment pamphleteer whose Common Sense and Rights of Man defend republicanism and natural rights.
Ethical concept of selfless concern for others' welfare, central to utilitarian and effective-altruist thought.
Political condition of statelessness at the center of anarchist theory rejecting the state's legitimacy.
Canadian philosopher whose Sources of the Self examines modern identity, and who offers a communitarian critique of liberal individualism.
Political-philosophical view that all humans belong to a single moral community, traced to the Stoics and Kant's "perpetual peace."
Ethical theory judging actions by adherence to duty or rules rather than consequences, epitomized by Kant.
American philosopher known for a compatibilist "hierarchical" theory of free will and the essay "On Bullshit."
Central question dividing Hobbes's pessimistic view from Rousseau's belief in a natural goodness corrupted by society.
Modern natural-rights framework tracing to Locke, articulated internationally after the Enlightenment and World War II.
Metaethical view that objective moral facts exist independent of human opinion or convention.
Bentham's design for a prison enabling constant potential surveillance, later analyzed by Foucault as a model of disciplinary power.
French anarchist theorist who declared "property is theft."
Political-philosophical topic on ownership, from Locke's labor theory of acquisition to Marx's critique and Proudhon's anarchism.
Central concept of political philosophy examining legitimacy, from Hobbes's Leviathan to Nozick's minimal state.
MacIntyre's work arguing modern moral discourse is fragmented and calling for a return to the Aristotelian virtue tradition.
Applied-ethics topic on the moral status of nonhuman animals, advanced by Peter Singer's Animal Liberation.
American Kantian ethicist whose Sources of Normativity addresses self-constitution and practical identity.
British Christian apologist (C. S. Lewis) whose Mere Christianity and The Abolition of Man defend objective morality.
Movement, associated with Peter Singer, applying rational analysis to maximize the good done by charitable giving.

entries.topic: Eichmann in Jerusalem: A Report on the Banality of Evil
entries.answerline: Eichmann in Jerusalem: A Report on the Banality of Evil
entries.note: Arendt's account of the trial of Adolf Eichmann, arguing evil can arise from thoughtless bureaucratic conformity.
Metaethical view that moral statements express emotional attitudes rather than factual claims, associated with Ayer.
Central political value debated across egalitarian, libertarian, and Rawlsian theories of justice.
Contemporary political-philosophical topic on justice, sovereignty, and cosmopolitanism across borders.
Australian philosopher whose Ethics: Inventing Right and Wrong argues for a moral error theory denying objective values exist.
French counter-Enlightenment political theorist defending monarchy, tradition, and divine providence against revolution.
Ghanaian-British-American philosopher of cosmopolitanism and identity; author of Cosmopolitanism: Ethics in a World of Strangers.
Russian novelist whose Christian anarchism and pacifist moral philosophy influenced Gandhi.
Distinguished from killing in debates over the doctrine of double effect and euthanasia.
American political philosopher known for Just and Unjust Wars and Spheres of Justice's communitarian pluralism.
Russian anarchist theorist who opposed Marx's state socialism in favor of stateless collectivism.
Political-philosophical topic on accommodating cultural diversity within liberal democratic states.
Feminist ethical concept of treating a person as a mere object or instrument, notably analyzed by Martha Nussbaum.
American contractualist ethicist whose What We Owe to Each Other grounds morality in principles no one could reasonably reject.
Central topic of axiology and metaethics on what things are worth pursuing or protecting.
Isaiah Berlin's essay distinguishing negative from positive liberty.
Nazi bureaucrat whose trial Arendt analyzed as exemplifying the "banality of evil."
Philosopher whose After Virtue argues modern moral discourse is fragmented and calls for a return to Aristotelian virtue.
French political theorist whose Democracy in America analyzed egalitarianism's promises and dangers, such as majority tyranny.
Analyzed by Arendt's Origins of Totalitarianism and Sartre's Anti-Semite and Jew as a form of political and existential bad faith.
Russian-American novelist-philosopher of Objectivism, championing rational self-interest and laissez-faire capitalism.
Home to Paulo Freire, whose Pedagogy of the Oppressed shaped critical, liberatory education theory.
Irish-British statesman and founder of modern conservatism who critiqued the French Revolution in his Reflections.
Nussbaum and Sen's framework measuring well-being by real freedoms to achieve valued functionings.
American philosopher whose Race Matters blends pragmatism, Christianity, and critical race theory.
Environmental philosophy, from Arne Naess, valuing nature intrinsically rather than merely for human use.
Metaethical view that morality is grounded in God's commands, raised by the Euthyphro dilemma.
Arendt's account of the trial of Adolf Eichmann and the "banality of evil."
Metaethical view, related to emotivism, that moral statements express attitudes rather than describe facts.
French syndicalist theorist whose Reflections on Violence argues for the mobilizing power of political myth.
Central undefinable term in Moore's Principia Ethica, whose meaning grounds all of ethics.
Mill's principle that individual liberty may only be restricted to prevent harm to others.
Central topic of political philosophy addressed by Rousseau's Discourse on Inequality and Rawlsian justice theory.
Irish-British philosopher and novelist; The Sovereignty of Good argues moral attention involves unselfing toward reality.
Israeli-British legal philosopher known for the service conception of authority and work in legal positivism.
Political theorist who argued cruelty should be considered the worst vice, grounding a "liberalism of fear."
Doctrine, rooted in Aquinas and Grotius, setting conditions for justly entering and conducting war.
Central subject of legal philosophy, from natural law theory to Hart and Austin's legal positivism.
Central subject of legal philosophy, from natural law theory to Hart and Austin's legal positivism.
Political philosophy centered on individual liberty, rights, and limited government, from Locke and Mill to Rawls.
Subject of "moral luck," from Bernard Williams and Nagel, on how factors outside an agent's control affect moral judgment.
German sociologist; the "Protestant work ethic" and the "iron cage" of modern rational-bureaucratic disenchantment.
English utilitarian whose On Liberty defends individual freedom via the harm principle.
Central subject of ethics, encompassing deontological, consequentialist, and virtue-based theories of right action.
Ethical and legal theory holding moral norms are grounded in human nature, developed by Aquinas and Grotius.
Regime whose totalitarianism Arendt analyzed and under which Heidegger's political entanglement remains controversial.

entries.topic: Reflections on the Revolution in France
entries.answerline: Reflections on the Revolution in France
entries.note: Burke's critique of the French Revolution, foundational to modern conservatism.
Subject of moral and political philosophy on how uncertain harms should be weighed and distributed.
Version of utilitarianism judging actions by whether they conform to rules that, if generally followed, maximize utility.
Subject of Camus's Myth of Sisyphus, which calls it "the one truly serious philosophical problem."
American sociologist whose structural functionalism drew on Weberian social theory to model social order.
Arendt's work distinguishing labor, work, and action as fundamental categories of human activity.
Arendt's work analyzing totalitarianism as a modern form of rule effacing the distinction between public and private.
Scottish essayist-historian who proposed the "great man theory" of history in On Heroes and Hero-Worship.
Subject of Sorel's Reflections on Violence and Fanon's account of anticolonial struggle.
American sociologist-philosopher whose "double consciousness" describes the divided self-perception of Black Americans.
Full name of the American sociologist-philosopher known for the concept of "double consciousness."
English philosopher whose Political Justice is considered the first systematic exposition of philosophical anarchism.

sections.name: Analytic Philosophy, Language & Pragmatism
sections.blurb: The Anglo-American tradition from Frege and Russell through logical positivism, ordinary language philosophy, formal semantics, and pragmatism.

entries.note: Austrian-British philosopher of the Tractatus and later Philosophical Investigations; language games and the private language argument.
British logician; co-authored Principia Mathematica with Whitehead; theory of descriptions developed in "On Denoting."
American analytic philosopher; "Two Dogmas of Empiricism" attacked the analytic-synthetic distinction; naturalized epistemology.
American pragmatist and psychologist; Pragmatism and The Varieties of Religious Experience; the "will to believe."
German logician; founder of modern logic and philosophy of language; sense/reference distinction in "On Sense and Reference."
American pragmatist; instrumentalism and influential work on education and democracy.
American founder of pragmatism; the pragmatic maxim in "How to Make Our Ideas Clear"; pioneer of semiotics.
American movement holding truth is what "works" practically; founded by Peirce, developed by James and Dewey.
American analytic philosopher; Naming and Necessity argues names are rigid designators, defends a posteriori necessity.
Formal study of valid inference; syllogistic from Aristotle, symbolic logic from Frege and Russell.
Wittgenstein's posthumous later work rejecting the Tractatus's picture theory for language games and meaning-as-use.
Logical positivist group including Carnap and Schlick holding meaningful statements must be empirically verifiable.
Austrian logician; incompleteness theorems proving formal systems cannot prove all truths within themselves.
British ordinary-language philosopher; The Concept of Mind attacked Cartesian dualism as the "ghost in the machine."
Austrian-British philosopher of science; falsifiability as the demarcation criterion in The Logic of Scientific Discovery.
American neopragmatist; Philosophy and the Mirror of Nature rejected representationalist epistemology.
American philosopher of language and mind; anomalous monism and the theory of radical interpretation.
American philosopher of mind and language; the Chinese Room argument against strong AI, and speech act theory.
Philosophy-of-language topic on reference; Kripke's rigid designators versus Frege and Russell's descriptivist theories.
British-American philosopher who co-wrote Principia Mathematica with Russell; founder of process philosophy.
American philosopher; Twin Earth thought experiment and semantic externalism, "meanings just ain't in the head."
Philosophy of language's central question; theories range from reference to use, as in Wittgenstein, to verification.
Kripke's lectures establishing names as rigid designators and defending necessary a posteriori truths.
Wittgenstein's early work proposing the picture theory of language, ending "whereof one cannot speak, thereof one must be silent."
Polish logician; semantic theory of truth and the T-schema, as in "'snow is white' is true iff snow is white."
British analytic philosopher; Principia Ethica's naturalistic fallacy and the "here is a hand" proof of the external world.
Spanish-American philosopher noted for "those who cannot remember the past are condemned to repeat it."
German-American logical positivist of the Vienna Circle; the verification principle and elimination of metaphysics.
American analytic philosopher who attacked the analytic-synthetic distinction and naturalized epistemology.
British ordinary-language philosopher whose How to Do Things with Words founded speech act theory.
Central topic of 20th-century analytic philosophy; meaning, reference, and use from Frege to Wittgenstein.
American philosopher whose "grue" paradox challenges induction; author of Ways of Worldmaking.
American linguist-philosopher; universal grammar and nativism in language acquisition; also a political critic of media.
Russell's 1905 paper introducing the theory of descriptions to resolve puzzles about non-referring names.
G. E. Moore's appeal to common-sense certainties, such as "here is a hand," against radical skepticism.
American philosopher of mind; the language-of-thought hypothesis and modularity of mind.
Central topic of 20th-century analytic philosophy; meaning, reference, and use from Frege to Wittgenstein.
Full name of the Austrian-British philosopher of the Tractatus and Philosophical Investigations.
Logical devices such as "all" and "some," formalized in Frege's predicate logic.
Quine's paper attacking the analytic-synthetic distinction and reductionism in empiricism.
Austrian philosopher who held nonexistent objects have a kind of being, a target of Russell's theory of descriptions.
American analytic philosopher whose modal realism holds possible worlds exist as concretely as the actual world.
British ordinary-language philosopher whose How to Do Things with Words founded speech act theory.
Wittgenstein's later concept that meaning arises from the rule-governed social use of words.
Austin and Searle's theory distinguishing the locutionary, illocutionary, and perlocutionary dimensions of utterances.
City that hosted the Vienna Circle of logical positivists.
American analytic philosopher whose modal realism holds possible worlds exist as concretely as the actual world.
Full name of the German logician who founded modern logic and philosophy of language.
British analytic philosopher; Principia Ethica's naturalistic fallacy and the "here is a hand" proof of the external world.
Context-dependent terms like "I," "here," and "now" studied in philosophy of language.
A. J. Ayer's manifesto of logical positivism, applying the verification principle to dismiss metaphysics and ethics as meaningless.
Philosophy of mathematics debates Platonism, logicism as in Frege and Russell, and formalism as in Hilbert.
British analytic philosopher known for anti-realism about meaning and histories of analytic philosophy.
Formal logic of necessity and possibility, developed using Kripke's possible-worlds semantics.
Basic valid inference rule: from "if P then Q" and "P," infer "Q."
Russell and Whitehead's work attempting to derive all of mathematics from logical axioms.
American Transcendentalist essayist whose "Self-Reliance" celebrates individual intuition over social conformity.
Quine's "radical translation" and the indeterminacy of meaning thought experiment.
American philosopher who attacked the "myth of the given" and framed the "space of reasons."
William James's essay defending the right to religious belief without sufficient evidence when the stakes are live and forced.
French founder of positivism and sociology; proposed a three-stage law of history from theological to scientific thought.
Full name of the British logician who co-authored Principia Mathematica.

entries.topic: Bertrand Arthur William Russell, 3rd Earl Russell
entries.answerline: Bertrand Arthur William Russell, 3rd Earl Russell
entries.note: Full name and title of the British logician who co-authored Principia Mathematica.
Austrian physicist-philosopher whose positivist critique of unobservable entities influenced the Vienna Circle.
Full name of the American philosopher behind the Chinese Room argument and speech act theory.
American absolute idealist and early pragmatist; The World and the Individual on loyalty and community.
School holding meaningful statements must be empirically verifiable, centered on the Vienna Circle.
Modal concept contrasted with contingency, central to Kripke's necessary a posteriori truths.
Modal concept contrasted with contingency, central to Kripke's necessary a posteriori truths.
Philosophical view restricting genuine knowledge to empirically verifiable, scientific claims, from Comte to the Vienna Circle.
Logical system extending first-order logic by allowing quantification over properties and relations, not just objects.
American university long associated with analytic philosophy of language and logic; hosts the Stanford Encyclopedia of Philosophy.
Knowledge or justification independent of experience, contrasted with a posteriori knowledge; central to Kant's synthetic a priori.
Mill's work on inductive and deductive reasoning and the methods of scientific inference.
British logical positivist whose Language, Truth, and Logic applies the verification principle against metaphysics.
Subject of Frege's logicist project to derive number theory from pure logic.
German-American philosopher of science known for the deductive-nomological model of explanation and the raven paradox.
Searle's thought experiment arguing symbol manipulation alone cannot produce genuine understanding, against strong AI.
Popper's criterion that a genuinely scientific theory must be capable of being proven false.
Logical system allowing degrees of truth between 0 and 1, addressing problems of vagueness.
British philosopher of language whose The Varieties of Reference treats demonstrative and indexical thought.
Ryle's distinction between practical "knowing how" and propositional "knowing that."
Classical logical principle that every proposition is either true or false, with no third option.
Self-referential paradox, "this sentence is false," challenging classical theories of truth.
20th-century shift making language the central object of philosophical analysis, associated with Frege, Russell, and Wittgenstein.
David Lewis's view that possible worlds exist as concretely as the actual world.
Subject of philosophy of mathematics debating whether numbers are abstract Platonic objects or human constructions.
English university associated with ordinary-language philosophy through Austin, Ryle, and Strawson.

entries.topic: Philosophy and the Mirror of Nature
entries.answerline: Philosophy and the Mirror of Nature
entries.note: Rorty's work rejecting representationalist epistemology's "mirror" metaphor for the mind.

entries.topic: Pragmatism: A New Name For Some Old Ways of Thinking
entries.answerline: Pragmatism: A New Name For Some Old Ways of Thinking
entries.note: William James's lectures popularizing pragmatism as a method for settling metaphysical disputes.
Philosophy-of-language topic on reference, debated between Frege and Russell's descriptivism and Kripke's causal theory.
Mental states like belief and desire directed at propositions, central to philosophy of mind and language.
Kripke's invented function used in his skeptical reading of Wittgenstein on rule-following.
Philosophy-of-language topic on how words connect to objects in the world, central to Frege, Russell, and Kripke.
Putnam's Twin Earth-based view that meaning depends on factors external to the mind.
Immediate objects of perception posited by early-20th-century epistemologists like Russell and Moore.
British philosopher P. F. Strawson, known for descriptive metaphysics and a critique of Russell's theory of descriptions.
Logically true statement true in virtue of its form alone, as analyzed in Wittgenstein's Tractatus.
Ryle's work attacking Cartesian dualism as the "ghost in the machine."
Putnam's thought experiment supporting semantic externalism, that meaning depends on factors beyond the mind.
Logical property of an argument whose conclusion follows necessarily from its premises, regardless of truth.
Full name of the American philosopher who attacked the "myth of the given."
Quine's essay proposing epistemology be replaced by empirical psychology.
Peirce's essay stating the pragmatic maxim for clarifying the meaning of concepts.
Frege's paper distinguishing a term's sense from its reference.
Peirce's essay on the methods of settling belief, favoring the scientific method.
McTaggart's paper arguing time, via the A- and B-series, does not truly exist.
British logical positivist whose Language, Truth, and Logic applies the verification principle against metaphysics.
Formal device in possible-worlds semantics determining which worlds are relevant to evaluating modal claims.
Philosophy-of-language topic on expressions with multiple possible meanings, relevant to logical formalization.
Refers to the analytic-synthetic distinction or the analytic tradition of 20th-century philosophy.
20th-century Anglo-American tradition emphasizing logical analysis and clarity, founded by Frege, Russell, and Moore.
Davidson's view that mental events are physical but not reducible to strict physical laws.
American legal philosopher and Nietzsche scholar known for the influential Leiter Reports philosophy rankings blog.
Logical "if-then" statements studied in formal and philosophical logic, including counterfactual conditionals.
British philosopher of language and mathematics known for neo-Fregean logicism and work on vagueness.
German mathematician whose formalist program sought to secure mathematics' foundations through finitary proof.
Russell's theory analyzing definite descriptions like "the present King of France" to resolve reference puzzles.
"Donkey sentences," such as "every farmer who owns a donkey beats it," pose puzzles for formal semantic theories of anaphora.
Errors in reasoning studied in informal and formal logic, from equivocation to affirming the consequent.
Wittgenstein's example of a "family resemblance" concept lacking a single defining essence, as in language games.
English mathematician-logician whose Boolean algebra formalized logical operations, foundational to modern logic and computing.
Godel's proof that formal systems cannot prove all truths within themselves.
Australian logician who defends dialetheism, the view that some contradictions are true.
Goodman's invented predicates illustrating the "new riddle of induction."
American university long central to pragmatism, home to James, Royce, Quine, Rawls, and Putnam.

entries.topic: How To Do Things With Words
entries.answerline: How To Do Things With Words
entries.note: Austin's work founding speech act theory by distinguishing locutionary, illocutionary, and perlocutionary acts.
Central to philosophy of action and to Anscombe's influential work of the same name.
Central to philosophy of action and to Anscombe's influential work Intention.
Full name of the Austrian-British philosopher of science known for falsifiability as the demarcation criterion.
Classical logical principle that every proposition is either true or false, with no third option.
English logician and author whose "What the Tortoise Said to Achilles" poses a regress problem for logical inference.
Logical "if-then" connective, defined truth-functionally, whose divergence from ordinary conditionals raises philosophical puzzles.
Brouwer's philosophy of mathematics holding mathematical objects are mental constructions, rejecting the law of excluded middle.
View that mathematical objects and truths exist independently of human minds, opposing formalism and constructivism.
Philosophy-of-language topic on figurative meaning, debated between literalist and conceptual (Lakoff and Johnson) theories.
Austrian founder of the Vienna Circle, murdered by a former student, a key figure of logical positivism.
Logical operator central to classical and non-classical logics, including debates over the law of excluded middle.
American philosopher of mathematics whose dilemma questions how abstract mathematical objects could be known.
Full name of the British philosopher known for descriptive metaphysics and a critique of Russell's theory of descriptions.
Home to the Lvov-Warsaw school of logicians, including Tarski and Lukasiewicz.
Philosophy-of-language concept for background assumptions a statement takes for granted, as in Strawson's critique of Russell.
Logical principle that a contradiction entails any statement whatsoever, motivating paraconsistent logics.
Central to Whitehead's process philosophy, treating reality as constituted by events rather than static substances.
