Whitehead's metaphysics treating reality as constituted by events and becoming rather than static substances.
Logical device using quantifiers like "all" and "some," formalized in Frege's predicate logic.
Hempel's puzzle showing observing a non-black non-raven seemingly confirms "all ravens are black."
Central to Hempel's raven paradox on confirmation of universal generalizations.
Kripke's term for names that refer to the same object in every possible world.
American philosopher whose Making It Explicit develops an inferentialist theory of meaning rooted in social practices.
Central to Wittgenstein's rule-following considerations and Kripke's skeptical interpretation of them.
American logician who pioneered quantified modal logic and a direct-reference theory of names before Kripke.
Logical property of a valid argument whose premises are also true.
American philosopher bridging ordinary-language philosophy and film and literary criticism; work on skepticism and Wittgenstein.
Semantic theory for handling vagueness by considering all admissible ways of making a term precise.

entries.topic: The Open Society and Its Enemies
entries.answerline: The Open Society and Its Enemies
entries.note: Popper's work critiquing historicism and totalitarian thought in Plato, Hegel, and Marx.
Likely references tree diagrams used in formal logic and syntax to represent structure.
Putnam's thought experiment supporting semantic externalism, that meaning depends on factors beyond the mind.
American university associated with influential 20th-century philosophers and the Chicago school of thought.
Quine's work introducing the indeterminacy of translation and the "gavagai" thought experiment.

sections.name: Philosophy of Mind, Epistemology & Science
sections.blurb: Theories of knowledge, mind, and scientific method, from Gettier problems to functionalism and the philosophy of science.

entries.note: Core epistemological concept; correspondence, coherence, and pragmatist theories dispute what makes a proposition true.
Debate over whether choices are undetermined; compatibilist and libertarian positions oppose hard determinism.
Traditionally defined as justified true belief; central topic of epistemology, complicated by Gettier problems.
Metaphysical relation Hume reduced to mere constant conjunction; central to philosophy of science and metaphysics.
Metaphysical topic covering McTaggart's A- and B-series, presentism versus eternalism, and time's relation to change.
Central problem of philosophy of mind; qualia and the "hard problem" of subjective experience.
Branch of philosophy studying the fundamental nature of reality, being, and existence.
Epistemological position doubting the possibility of certain knowledge; from ancient Pyrrhonism to Cartesian doubt.
American philosopher of mind; "What Is It Like to Be a Bat?" argues subjective experience resists physicalist reduction.
American philosopher of mind; Consciousness Explained's multiple drafts model and the intentional stance.
Denial of God's existence, debated via the problem of evil and against classical theistic proofs.
Modal-logic device for analyzing necessity and possibility; David Lewis's modal realism treats them as concretely existing.
Branch of philosophy studying the nature, sources, and limits of knowledge and justified belief.
Inference from particular observations to general conclusions; Hume's problem questions its rational justification.
Philosophy of science examines demarcation, method, and theory change, notably in Popper and Kuhn.
Austrian founder of psychoanalysis; id, ego, superego, and the unconscious; widely influential on continental philosophy.
View that mind and body are distinct substances, classically defended by Descartes.
View, classically Cartesian, that mind and body are separate substances that interact causally.
Subjective, qualitative properties of experience central to the "hard problem" of consciousness.
Propositional attitude central to epistemology's definition of knowledge as justified true belief.
Conditional statements about what would have been the case; central to David Lewis's possible-worlds semantics.
Propositional attitudes central to epistemology's definition of knowledge as justified true belief.
View that reality, or mind and body, ultimately consists of a single substance, opposing dualism.
Polish astronomer whose heliocentric model prompted philosophical debate over scientific revolutions and cosmology.
Philosophy of mind topic on machine cognition; the Turing test and Searle's Chinese Room challenge to strong AI.
Epistemological topic on how sense experience yields knowledge of the external world; direct versus representative realism.
American philosopher of science; The Structure of Scientific Revolutions and incommensurable paradigm shifts.
English naturalist whose theory of evolution by natural selection reshaped the philosophy of biology and mind.
Philosophy-of-perception topic on whether color is a mind-independent property or a secondary quality.
Position that free will and determinism are mutually consistent, defended by Hume and later Harry Frankfurt.
Metaphysical concept central to debates over what it means for something "to be," from Parmenides to Sartre.
Philosophy-of-mind view that mental states are defined by their causal role rather than their physical substrate.
Relation whereby higher-level properties, such as the mental, depend on but aren't reducible to lower-level physical properties.
Kuhn's 1962 work introducing paradigm shifts and incommensurability in scientific change.
Metaphysical problem of whether general properties exist independently of particulars; realism versus nominalism.
Australian philosopher who coined "the hard problem of consciousness" and discusses philosophical zombies.
American philosopher whose 1963 paper produced counterexamples showing justified true belief isn't sufficient for knowledge.
Subject of philosophy of mind's debates over consciousness, dualism, and physicalism.
Logical and semantic problem of borderline cases, exemplified by the sorites paradox.
American analytic philosopher of religion; modal ontological argument and "warranted Christian belief."
Formal study of the parthood relations between wholes and their parts.
Topic in epistemology and philosophy of science quantifying uncertainty, central to Pascal's wager and inductive reasoning.

entries.topic: "What is it like to be a bat?"
entries.answerline: "What is it like to be a bat?"
entries.note: Nagel's paper arguing subjective conscious experience resists objective physical description.
Metaphysical topic covering personal identity over time and Leibniz's identity of indiscernibles.
English physicist whose absolute space and time framework was debated against Leibniz's relationalism.
Thought experiment, from Chalmers, imagining physical duplicates lacking conscious experience, challenging physicalism.
View that higher-level phenomena can be explained fully in terms of more basic constituents.
Systematic empirical inquiry theorized through Bacon's induction and later Popper's falsificationism.
Physicist whose relativity theory prompted philosophical debate over the nature of space and time.
Full name of the American analytic philosopher of religion known for the modal ontological argument.
Epistemological theory of justification holding beliefs are justified by mutual support within a system, opposing foundationalism.
View that all events, including choices, are fully caused by prior states, raising the free will debate.
View that meaning or mental content depends partly on factors external to the individual, as in Putnam's Twin Earth.
Hungarian-British philosopher of science whose research-programmes methodology refined Popper's falsificationism.
Thesis that mental states can be realized by different physical substrates, supporting functionalism over reductive physicalism.
Paradigm case in philosophy of mind for discussing qualia and the mind-body problem.
Kuhn's concept of revolutionary changes in scientific frameworks that are incommensurable with what preceded them.
View that scientific theories describe a mind-independent reality, including unobservable entities.
Metaphysical topic on personal identity, from Locke's memory criterion to Hume's "bundle" of perceptions.
Metaphysical relation Hume reduced to mere constant conjunction; central to philosophy of science.
Full name of the American philosopher of mind behind Consciousness Explained and the intentional stance.
Basic category of empiricist epistemology and phenomenology's analysis of consciousness.
Contemporary movement using empirical surveys to test the intuitions behind philosophical thought experiments.
Central to Locke's criterion of personal identity and to epistemological debates over knowledge of the past.
American philosopher of mind distinguishing access consciousness from phenomenal consciousness.
Branch of metaphysics studying what kinds of things exist and how they are categorized.
Kuhn's term for the governing frameworks of normal science, replaced in scientific revolutions.
Metaphysical problem of what makes a person the same over time; Locke's memory theory versus Parfit's psychological continuity.
French physicist-philosopher; the Duhem-Quine thesis holds scientific hypotheses can only be tested in bundles, not in isolation.
British evolutionary biologist known for The Selfish Gene and prominent public arguments for atheism.
Metaphysical topic on absolute versus relational views, debated between Newton and Leibniz and reshaped by Kant.
British mathematician who proposed the Turing test for machine intelligence.
Philosophy-of-mind view, associated with Ryle, that mental states are reducible to behavioral dispositions.
Central to philosophy-of-mind debates over machine cognition, from the Turing test to the Chinese Room.
Dennett's book proposing the "multiple drafts" model of consciousness against a single Cartesian theater.
Popper's problem of distinguishing genuine science from pseudoscience via falsifiability.
Freudian concept of the rational, reality-mediating part of the psyche between id and superego.
View, associated with the Churchlands, that folk-psychological concepts like belief will be eliminated by a mature neuroscience.
Metaphysical category studied in Davidson's theory of action and causation.
Darwin's theory of natural selection, prompting philosophical debate over teleology, mind, and the biological origins of ethics.
Counterexamples, from Edmund Gettier, showing justified true belief isn't sufficient for knowledge.
Epistemological source of immediate, non-inferential knowledge, central to rationalist and phenomenological method.
Korean-American philosopher of mind whose exclusion argument challenges non-reductive physicalism's account of mental causation.
Metaphysical category studied in debates over universals, particulars, and mereology.
Epistemological problem of how one can know that other beings have conscious experiences.
Physical theory prompting philosophical debate over determinism, realism, and the nature of measurement.
American epistemologist and metaphysician known for foundationalist theory of knowledge and the problem of the criterion.
English physicist whose absolute space and time framework was debated against Leibniz's relationalism.
Skeptical position that only one's own mind can be known to exist.
"Paradox of the heap," illustrating the problem of vagueness through incremental changes.
"Paradox of the heap," illustrating the problem of vagueness through incremental changes.
Metaphysical topic on absolute versus relational views, debated between Newton and Leibniz.
English statistician whose theorem underlies Bayesian confirmation theory in epistemology.
Studied in philosophy of perception as a paradigm case of how sense data relate to external objects.
Subject of philosophy of action examining intention, causation, and what distinguishes doings from mere happenings.
Those who deny God's existence, a position argued via the problem of evil and critiques of theistic proofs.
Hume's view that the self is nothing but a bundle of perceptions with no underlying substantial unity.
Full name of the English naturalist whose theory of evolution reshaped philosophy of biology and mind.
Surname of Paul and Patricia Churchland, proponents of eliminative materialism about folk psychology.
Full name of the American philosopher whose counterexamples showed justified true belief isn't sufficient for knowledge.
Concept, from Miranda Fricker, describing wrongs done to someone specifically in their capacity as a knower.
Epistemological theory holding justified beliefs rest on basic, self-justifying foundational beliefs.
Interpretation of probability defining it as the long-run frequency of an event across repeated trials.
Chalmers's term for explaining why physical processes give rise to subjective experience at all.
Inference from particular observations to general conclusions; Hume's problem questions its rational justification.
Problem in epistemology where justifying a belief requires justifying another belief, ad infinitum, motivating foundationalism.
Dennett's term for thought experiments designed to elicit and shape philosophical intuitions.
American experimental philosopher known for the "Knobe effect" on the role of moral judgment in ordinary concept use.
View that philosophy should be continuous with natural science, opposing supernatural or a priori explanations.
View, associated with Colin McGinn, that the hard problem of consciousness may be permanently unsolvable by human minds.
Epistemological ideal of judgment independent of individual perspective or bias, contrasted with subjectivity.
American philosopher known for skeptical arguments and later work defending moral obligations to the distant poor.
American metaphysician known for work on free will, material composition, and the "special composition question."
View that everything, including the mind, is ultimately physical.
Discipline historically intertwined with philosophy of mind, from Aristotle's De Anima to William James.
Metaphysical view that a mind-independent external world exists, opposed to idealism and anti-realism.
Metaphysical strategy of explaining higher-level phenomena entirely in terms of more basic constituents.
Epistemological theory holding a belief is justified if produced by a reliable cognitive process.
Freudian concept of unconsciously excluding unacceptable thoughts or desires from awareness.
Relates to philosophy of science's inquiry into method, explanation, and theory change.
Dennett's derisive term for miraculous, unexplained sources of design, contrasted with gradual "cranes" like evolution.
American philosopher of mind known for work on personal identity and the causal theory of properties.
Invoked in Eddington's "two tables" problem, contrasting the manifest solid table with its scientific image as mostly empty space.
Method using imagined scenarios, like trolley problems and Twin Earth, to test philosophical intuitions and theories.
Central predicate analyzed by correspondence, coherence, and deflationary theories of truth.
Modal-logic device for analyzing necessity and possibility; David Lewis's modal realism treats them as concretely existing.

sections.name: Phenomenology & Existentialism
sections.blurb: Consciousness, being, and lived experience as examined by Husserl, Heidegger, Sartre, and their existentialist successors.

entries.note: German phenomenologist of Being and Time; analyzed Dasein's being-toward-death; influenced existentialism and hermeneutics.
French existentialist; The Second Sex argues "one is not born, but becomes, a woman."
French existentialist; Being and Nothingness holds "existence precedes essence"; radical freedom and bad faith.
Method founded by Husserl studying the structures of consciousness through bracketing, or epoche.
German founder of phenomenology; the intentionality of consciousness and the transcendental ego.
Theory of interpretation, especially of texts; developed by Schleiermacher and Dilthey, culminating in Gadamer's Truth and Method.
Heidegger's 1927 work analyzing Dasein's temporal, thrown existence toward death.
French phenomenologist of embodiment; Phenomenology of Perception on the lived body.
Beauvoir's 1949 foundational feminist existentialist work on woman as constructed "Other."
Heidegger's term, literally "being-there," for the particular kind of being that questions its own existence.
French phenomenologist for whom ethics is "first philosophy"; the face of the Other demands infinite responsibility.
French vitalist philosopher of elan vital and the distinction between clock time and lived duration (duree).
Camus's essay on the absurd, concluding "one must imagine Sisyphus happy."
Existential theme central to Heidegger's being-toward-death and Epicurus's claim that death is "nothing to us."
Home to Descartes's rationalism, Enlightenment philosophes, and the 20th-century existentialist and post-structuralist movements.
Husserl's term, borrowed from Brentano, for consciousness's directedness toward objects.
Existentialist theme; Sartre's Being and Nothingness treats consciousness as a "nothing" that negates being.
20th-century movement holding "existence precedes essence"; radical freedom and responsibility, led by Sartre and Camus.
French-Algerian philosopher of the absurd; The Myth of Sisyphus and The Stranger.
Central question of Heidegger's Being and Time, which asks after the "meaning of Being" itself.
German hermeneutic philosopher whose Truth and Method describes the "fusion of horizons" in interpretation.
Concept of alterity central to Levinas's ethics and Beauvoir's account of woman as Other.
Sartre's term for self-deception in which one denies one's own radical freedom.
Full name of the German founder of phenomenology and the intentionality of consciousness.
Central theme of Merleau-Ponty's phenomenology of embodied perception.
Gadamer's hermeneutic work describing the "fusion of horizons" in interpretation.
Sartre's major work holding "existence precedes essence" and analyzing radical freedom and bad faith.

entries.topic: Being and Nothingness: An Essay on Phenomenological Ontology
entries.answerline: Being and Nothingness: An Essay on Phenomenological Ontology
entries.note: Full title of Sartre's major existentialist work on freedom and bad faith.
Heidegger's term, Sorge, for the fundamental structure of Dasein's being-in-the-world.
Metaphysical concept contrasted with existence, central to Sartre's "existence precedes essence."
Martin Buber's work distinguishing the relational "I-Thou" encounter from the objectifying "I-It."
Jewish philosopher whose I and Thou distinguishes the relational "I-Thou" encounter from the objectifying "I-It."
Subject of Heidegger's critique of technology's "enframing," which reduces nature to a mere resource.
Swiss Reformed theologian whose dialectical theology emphasized God's radical transcendence against liberal theology.
German-Swiss existentialist psychiatrist known for "boundary situations" and the "axial age" concept of world philosophy's parallel emergence.
German-American theologian whose "ultimate concern" and existentialist theology bridged Christianity and continental philosophy.
French mystic-philosopher whose Gravity and Grace treats affliction, attention, and self-emptying love.

entries.topic: "The Origin of the Work of Art"
entries.answerline: "The Origin of the Work of Art"
entries.note: Heidegger's essay on art's disclosure of truth.
Husserl's epoche, suspending judgment about the external world's existence to study consciousness itself.
Bergson's vital force driving creative evolution, contrasted with mechanistic explanations of life.
Austrian philosopher-psychologist whose concept of intentionality influenced his student Husserl's phenomenology.
German theologian-philosopher and founder of modern hermeneutics, emphasizing feeling in religion.
French philosopher of science and poetics whose The Poetics of Space examines the phenomenology of intimate spaces.
Full name of the French vitalist philosopher of elan vital and lived duration (duree).
Relating to hermeneutics, the theory of interpretation developed by Schleiermacher, Dilthey, and Gadamer.
Central theme of Merleau-Ponty's phenomenology of embodied perception.
Spanish philosopher; The Revolt of the Masses critiques mass society, with the maxim "I am I and my circumstance."
German phenomenologist of values and emotion; ranked a hierarchy of values from the sensory to the holy.

entries.topic: On Religion: Speeches to Its Cultured Despisers
entries.answerline: On Religion: Speeches to Its Cultured Despisers
entries.note: Schleiermacher's work defending religion as grounded in feeling rather than doctrine or reason.
French hermeneutic phenomenologist; Time and Narrative on the relation between narrative and lived temporal experience.
Home to E. M. Cioran, whose aphoristic works express a philosophy of pessimism and despair.

entries.topic: Totality and Infinity: An Essay on Exteriority
entries.answerline: Totality and Infinity: An Essay on Exteriority
entries.note: Levinas's work grounding ethics in the encounter with the infinite Other.
German philosopher who distinguished the human sciences (Geisteswissenschaften) from natural sciences via hermeneutic understanding.

sections.name: Post-structuralism & Critical Theory
sections.blurb: Twentieth-century French and German thought critiquing power, language, and ideology, from the Frankfurt School to deconstruction.

entries.note: French philosopher who founded deconstruction; critiqued logocentrism and coined differance in Of Grammatology.
French theorist of power and knowledge; Discipline and Punish and The History of Sexuality; panopticon and biopower.
Italian Marxist theorist; concept of cultural hegemony developed in the Prison Notebooks.
French philosopher of Difference and Repetition and (with Guattari) A Thousand Plateaus; the "body without organs."
Late-20th-century movement skeptical of grand narratives and objective truth; associated with Lyotard, Foucault, and Derrida.
German critical theorist of communicative action and discourse ethics; second-generation Frankfurt School.
American theorist whose Gender Trouble argues gender is performative rather than essential.
German critical theorist of "The Work of Art in the Age of Mechanical Reproduction," aura, and the flaneur.
German-American Frankfurt School theorist; One-Dimensional Man critiques consumer capitalism's false needs.
French psychoanalyst of the mirror stage who held "the unconscious is structured like a language."
Slovenian philosopher blending Lacanian psychoanalysis, Hegel, and Marxist ideology critique.
French theorist of simulacra and simulation, arguing media replaces reality with hyperreality.
German Frankfurt School theorist; Dialectic of Enlightenment, co-written with Horkheimer, and the "culture industry."
Martinique-born psychiatrist and anti-colonial theorist; Black Skin, White Masks and The Wretched of the Earth.
Bulgarian-French theorist of abjection and the semiotic/symbolic distinction in language and psychoanalysis.
Philosophical movement examining gender oppression, from Beauvoir's existentialist feminism to Butler's performativity.
Group of German critical theorists, including Adorno, Horkheimer, Marcuse, and Habermas, blending Marxism with cultural critique.
American Marxist critic who theorized postmodernism as "the cultural logic of late capitalism."
American literary critic known for "the anxiety of influence" theory of poetic tradition.
Marxist and critical-theory concept of false consciousness masking material interests, developed by Gramsci and Althusser.
Edward Said's critique of Western scholarly representations that construct the East as an exotic Other.
French literary theorist whose "The Death of the Author" argues meaning is independent of authorial intent.
Semiotic concept of signifier and signified central to Saussure's structuralism and its post-structuralist critique.
Foucault's multivolume work arguing sexuality is discursively constructed rather than merely repressed.
Subject of feminist philosophy from Wollstonecraft's rationalist argument to Beauvoir's and Butler's theories of gender.
Kristeva's concept of the horror provoked by what disturbs identity and order, such as bodily waste.
American theorist whose "A Cyborg Manifesto" blends feminism, technology, and posthumanism.
Italian philosopher whose "state of exception" and "bare life" (homo sacer) analyze sovereign power.
French postmodern philosopher whose The Postmodern Condition declares "incredulity toward metanarratives."
Study of signs and their meaning, founded by Saussure and Peirce, central to structuralist and post-structuralist theory.
French philosopher whose Being and Event grounds ontology in set theory and theorizes fidelity to truth-events.
Fanon's psychoanalytic study of colonialism's effects on Black identity and consciousness.
French sociologist-philosopher whose actor-network theory and science studies question the nature/society divide.
Adorno and Horkheimer's term for mass-produced culture that pacifies and standardizes consumers.
Central concept in Deleuze's Difference and Repetition and Derrida's differance, opposing philosophies of identity.
Postcolonial theorist whose "Can the Subaltern Speak?" questions whether marginalized voices can be represented.
Subject of feminist philosophy distinguishing biological sex from socially constructed gender roles.

entries.topic: Gender Trouble: Feminism and the Subversion of Identity
entries.answerline: Gender Trouble: Feminism and the Subversion of Identity
entries.note: Full title of Judith Butler's work arguing gender is performative rather than essential.
Hungarian Marxist theorist whose History and Class Consciousness introduced the concept of reification.
Concept of the historical present analyzed critically by theorists such as Habermas and Foucault.
Derrida's foundational deconstructionist work critiquing logocentrism and the privileging of speech over writing.
Concept developed by Austin and adapted by Butler holding that speech or identity can enact rather than merely describe.
Full name of the German Frankfurt School theorist of Dialectic of Enlightenment and the "culture industry."
Derrida's Of Grammatology reverses the traditional privileging of speech over writing.
American philosopher of art whose "artworld" concept and claims about "the end of art" followed Warhol's Brillo Boxes.
Foucault's concept for modern power's regulation of populations' bodies and biological life.
Central figure of Donna Haraway's "A Cyborg Manifesto," blending feminism and posthumanism.
Adorno and Horkheimer's work arguing Enlightenment rationality turns into a new form of myth and domination.

entries.topic: Discipline and Punish: The Birth of the Prison
entries.answerline: Discipline and Punish: The Birth of the Prison
entries.note: Foucault's work tracing the shift from corporal punishment to disciplinary surveillance, exemplified by the panopticon.
German Marxist philosopher whose The Principle of Hope theorizes utopian longing as a driving historical force.
German city that gave its name to the Frankfurt School of critical theorists.
Judith Butler's work arguing gender is performative rather than essential.
Theory, from George Dickie, holding an object is art because the "artworld" institution confers that status upon it.
Concept, from Kimberle Crenshaw, describing how overlapping social identities compound experiences of oppression.
Subject of Benjamin's and Barthes's analyses of mechanical reproduction and the photographic image's relation to reality.
Distinguished from gender in feminist philosophy; also central to Foucault's History of Sexuality.
Baudrillard's concept of copies without an original that replace and precede reality itself.
Foucault's methodological work on discourse, statements, and historical epistemes.
Fukuyama's Hegelian-inspired thesis that liberal democracy represents humanity's final form of government.
Foucault's archaeology of knowledge tracing shifting epistemes across the human sciences.
Italian semiotician-novelist who applied structuralist theory to interpretation and popular culture.
Full name of the German critical theorist of "The Work of Art in the Age of Mechanical Reproduction."
Derrida's essay critiquing Austin's speech act theory through the concept of iterability.
Helene Cixous's feminist manifesto calling for women's writing, or ecriture feminine.

entries.topic: "The Work of Art in the Age of Mechanical Reproduction"
entries.answerline: "The Work of Art in the Age of Mechanical Reproduction"
entries.note: Benjamin's essay on aura's loss through mechanical reproduction.

Deleuze and Guattari's concept of a body freed from organized, hierarchical structure.

Subject of philosophical film theory, including Deleuze's Cinema books on the movement-image and time-image.

Concept of objectifying observation theorized by Sartre, Lacan, and feminist film theory's "male gaze."
American historiographer whose Metahistory analyzes historical writing as governed by underlying narrative and rhetorical tropes.
Gramsci's concept of cultural and ideological dominance maintaining ruling-class power without overt coercion.
French Marxist theorist whose The Production of Space argues social space is actively produced rather than a neutral container.
Term used by Fredric Jameson to characterize postmodernism as its "cultural logic."
French feminist philosopher and psychoanalyst who critiqued phallocentrism in Western philosophical language.
Adorno's collection of aphorisms on damaged life under late capitalism.
Achille Mbembe's concept describing sovereign power's capacity to dictate who may live and who must die.
Marcuse's critique of advanced industrial society's suppression of critical, oppositional thought.
German philosopher of Critique of Cynical Reason and the "spheres" trilogy on human self-enclosure.

Lukacs's Marxist concept of social relations appearing as fixed, thing-like properties.

American critic whose "Notes on Camp" and Against Interpretation analyze aesthetics and cultural sensibility.

Betty Friedan's work diagnosing the unspoken malaise of mid-century domestic women.

sections.name: Non-Western Philosophy
sections.blurb: Chinese, Indian, Japanese, and African philosophical traditions and their major schools and thinkers.

entries.note: Chinese sage of ren and li recorded in the Analects; foundational to Confucianism and filial piety.
Confucian philosopher who argued human nature is innately good, needing only cultivation.
Daoist philosopher of the butterfly dream and the relativism of perspective in the eponymous text.
Home to Confucianism, Daoism, Legalism, and Mohism as competing schools of the Hundred Schools of Thought.
Chinese ethical-political tradition founded by Confucius emphasizing ren, ritual propriety, and filial piety.
Chinese statecraft school associated with Han Feizi, emphasizing strict law and punishment over Confucian virtue.
Indian tradition founded by the Buddha teaching the Four Noble Truths, anatta (no-self), and the end of suffering.
Chinese founder of Mohism who advocated impartial care and a utilitarian-like consequentialism against Confucian ritual.
Indian Buddhist philosopher; founder of the Madhyamaka school teaching the emptiness (sunyata) of inherent existence.
Compiled sayings of Confucius on ren, li, and the cultivation of the junzi, or gentleman.
Confucian virtue of respect and duty owed to parents and ancestors, central to the Analects.
Home to Hindu, Buddhist, and Jain philosophical traditions, including the Upanishads and Nagarjuna's Madhyamaka.
Chinese tradition founded by Laozi emphasizing the dao, wu wei (effortless action), and naturalness.
Japanese school led by Nishida Kitaro blending Zen Buddhism with Western phenomenology.
Indian school of logic and epistemology systematizing the valid means of knowledge, or pramana.
Hindu concept of the true self, held in the Upanishads to be identical with Brahman.
Home to Zen Buddhism and the Kyoto School's synthesis of Buddhist thought with Western phenomenology.
Chinese school founded by Mozi advocating impartial care and a consequentialist ethic.
Chinese tradition founded by Laozi emphasizing the dao, wu wei (effortless action), and naturalness.
Chinese Neo-Confucian philosopher who synthesized li (principle) and qi (vital force) into a systematic metaphysics.
Confucian philosopher who, against Mencius, argued human nature is innately bad and requires ritual cultivation.
Indian philosopher who systematized Advaita Vedanta, teaching the non-dual identity of atman and Brahman.
Home to a documented tradition of African philosophy, including the 17th-century rationalist Zera Yacob.
Confucian concept of ritual propriety, or in Neo-Confucianism, the metaphysical principle underlying all things.
Chinese synthesis of Confucian ethics with Buddhist and Daoist metaphysics, developed by Zhu Xi and Wang Yangming.
Indian epistemological term for valid means of knowledge, systematized especially in the Nyaya school.
Classical language of Indian philosophical texts including the Upanishads and Nyaya treatises.
Buddhist concept of emptiness, the lack of inherent existence in all phenomena, central to Nagarjuna's Madhyamaka.
Chinese Neo-Confucian philosopher who taught the unity of knowledge and action against Zhu Xi's rationalism.
Japanese Buddhist tradition emphasizing meditation and direct, non-conceptual insight over doctrinal study.
Continent whose philosophical traditions include Ubuntu ethics and modern figures like Appiah and Fanon.
Jaspers's term for the mid-first-millennium BCE period when Confucius, the Buddha, and Greek philosophers emerged in parallel.
Refers to Zhuangzi's famous dream of being a butterfly, questioning the boundary between dream and reality.
Central Daoist concept of the ineffable, natural way underlying and guiding all things.
West African nation home to modern philosophers including Kwame Anthony Appiah and Kwasi Wiredu.
Chinese sage traditionally credited with the Daodejing, founder of Daoism, teaching wu wei and the ineffable dao.
Buddhist tradition emphasizing the bodhisattva ideal and, in Nagarjuna's Madhyamaka, the emptiness of all phenomena.
Chinese revolutionary leader whose On Contradiction and On Practice adapted Marxist dialectics to Chinese conditions.
Confucian doctrine that social order requires words and titles to correspond accurately to reality and role.
Home to Hindu, Buddhist, and Jain philosophical traditions, including the Upanishads and Nagarjuna's Madhyamaka.
Ancient Indian philosophical texts teaching the identity of atman and Brahman, foundational to Vedanta.
Chinese philosopher who advocated radical self-preservation and individualism against Confucian and Mohist ethics.
Indian philosophical and practical school, one of six orthodox darsanas, aimed at liberation through disciplined meditation.
Daoist philosopher of the butterfly dream and the relativism of perspective in the eponymous text.
